text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist
Riverside Neurology Centre
St. Mark’s Hospital, Maiden Lane, London WC2E 7NA

Dear Dr A. Patel,

Mr Thomas Reed, DOB: 22-09-1992, Hospital No: K847210 NHS No. 2039847761
Flat 5, 12 Brookside Road, London, SW18 3PL

Diagnosis:

Generalised epilepsy with photosensitivity; prior trials of Sodium Valproate and Topiramate partially effective, current regimen Lamotrigine with adjunct Levetiracetam

Aetiology:

Idiopathic generalised epilepsy phenotype with documented photosensitivity on history; no structural lesion identified on previous MRI

Other Medical Problems:

Migraine with visual aura (intermittent), mild anxiety symptoms managed with guided self-help; vitamin D insufficiency corrected

Management Plan:

- Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg mane and 200 mg nocte; remain on a single brand
- Maintain Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily
- Given photosensitivity to flicker or bright light, patient has been counselled on practical avoidance strategies (screen flicker minimisation, use of blue-light filters, and avoiding strobe environments); he reports already wearing tinted lenses outdoors with perceived benefit
- Arrange routine EEG with photic stimulation and repeat serum Lamotrigine level prior to next visit
- Safety advice reinforced regarding drop attacks and generalised tonic-clonic seizures; he is using a fall-alert wearable at home
- Review in six months, earlier if seizure pattern changes or adverse effects emerge

Since his last appointment he reports being attentive to light-trigger avoidance at work (he has adjusted monitor refresh settings and brightness) and avoids venues with strobe lighting. Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal clonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. He attributes this to stricter trigger avoidance and better sleep routine, though he accepts this may be coincidental; we will continue to monitor.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal clonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequencies mentioned: 1) "he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month" → 1 to 2 per month. 2) "He has daily absences" → daily = 1 per day (at least). 3) "he experiences focal clonic with disorientation every three to four weeks" → roughly 1 per 3 to 4 weeks (~0.25 to 0.33 per week), which is less frequent than daily absences. 4) "together with drop attacks" without a frequency specified. 5) The sentence "Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported" conflicts with the immediately preceding paragraph stating ongoing seizures over the past six months; likely a documentation inconsistency. The instruction says to prioritize explicit numeric expressions and to choose the highest frequency when multiple exist. The highest explicit frequency is daily absences → at least 1 per day. Therefore, the final label is 1 per day.
reference: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal clonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported
